title: Isaiah 57
subtitle: The Contrite and Lowly in Spirit

sections.header: Verses 1–13 — “The Righteous Perish and No One Cares”

heb.paragraph: "The righteous (ṣaddîq) perish, and no one takes it to heart; the devout are taken away, and no one understands that the righteous are taken away to be spared from evil" (v.1). The righteous die unnoticed — but their death is protection, not punishment. Then a fierce condemnation of idolatry: child sacrifice in the valleys (v.5), libations poured to smooth stones in the ravines (v.6), adultery on every high hill (v.7). "Whom have you so dreaded and feared that you have not been true to me? Is it not because I have long been silent that you do not fear me?" (v.11). God silence was misread as absence.

notes.note: "The righteous perish and no one cares. They are taken away to be spared from evil." The paradox: the righteous death is mercy. God removes the faithful before the worst arrives.

"You sacrifice your children in the ravines. You pour out libations to smooth stones." Child sacrifice and river-stone worship — the darkest idolatry. The smooth stones are chosen by human aesthetics, not divine command.
"Is it not because I have long been silent that you do not fear me?" God silence during idolatry was not approval but patience. They mistook restraint for indifference.

sections.header: Verses 14–21 — “I Live in a High and Holy Place, but Also with the Contrite”

heb.paragraph: "I live in a high and holy place, but also with the one who is contrite (dakkāʾ) and lowly in spirit, to revive the spirit of the lowly and to revive the heart of the contrite" (v.15). THE verse of divine paradox: the highest God with the lowest people. Transcendence AND immanence in one sentence. "Build up, build up, prepare the road! Remove every obstacle out of the way of my people" (v.14). God creates peace for the far and the near (v.19). "But the wicked are like the tossing sea. There is no peace for the wicked" (vv.20–21) — the second section-divider (cf. 48:22).

notes.note: "I live in a high and holy place, but also with the one who is contrite and lowly in spirit." The most important verse in Third Isaiah. God inhabits two addresses: the highest heaven and the lowest heart. He goes down to go high.

"Peace, peace, to those far and near." Ephesians 2:17: "He came and preached peace to you who were far away and peace to those who were near." Paul reads this as Christ preaching to Gentiles (far) and Jews (near).
"The wicked are like the tossing sea. There is no peace for the wicked." The second section-ending refrain (cf. 48:22). The sea that cannot rest: restlessness as the defining condition of the wicked.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVWho are you mocking? At whom do you sneer and stick out your tongue? Are you not a brood of rebels, the offspring of liars?ESVWhom are you mocking? Against whom do you open your mouth wide and stick out your tongue? Are you not children of transgression, the offspring of deceit,NIVBehind your doors and your doorposts you have put your pagan symbols. Forsaking me, you uncovered your bed, you climbed into it and opened it wide; you made a pact with those whose beds you love, and you looked with lust on their naked bodies.ESVBehind the door and the doorpost you have set up your memorial; for, deserting me, you have uncovered your bed, you have gone up to it, you have made it wide; and you have made a covenant for yourself with them, you have loved their bed, you have looked on nakedness.

rec.text: ""The righteous perish and no one cares. They are taken away to be spared from evil." The paradox: the righteous death is mercy. God removes the faithful before the worst arrives."

rows.label: The Righteous Taken Away — 57:1–13
rows.text: "The righteous PERISH and no one cares" — but spared from evil. Child sacrifice condemned. God silence misread.

hebtext.note: "The righteous (ṣaddîq) perish, and no one takes it to heart; the devout are taken away, and no one understands that the righteous are taken away to be spared from evil" (v.1). The righteous die unnoticed — but their death is protection, not punishment. "Whom have you so dreaded and feared that you have not been true to me? Is it not because I have long been silent that you do not fear me?" (v.11). God silence was misread as absence.

"I live in a high and holy place, but also with the one who is contrite (dakkāʾ) and lowly in spirit, to revive the spirit of the lowly and to revive the heart of the contrite" (v.15). THE verse of divine paradox: the highest God with the lowest people. Transcendence AND immanence in one sentence. "Build up, build up, prepare the road! There is no peace for the wicked" (vv.20–21) — the second section-divider (cf. 48:22).

themes.note: Holiness peaks at 9: the high-and-holy God with the contrite. Mercy: peace to far and near (Eph 2:17). The divine paradox: transcendence inhabits lowliness.
